food_subgroup: Soft-cooked eggs with soy sauce and white pepper

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has an egg food product concept (FOODON_03301707); the Singapore-style half-boiled egg — runny whites, liquid yolk, served in a bowl with dark soy sauce and white pepper — is a specific traditional preparation without an exact FoodOn concept. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A09CB covers soft-boiled eggs as the closest parent; no specific code exists for the Singapore kopitiam half-boiled preparation with soy sauce and white pepper.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept found for Singapore-style half-boiled eggs as a specific preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 2 half-boiled eggs broken into a small bowl (approximately 130 g), seasoned with a few drops of dark soy sauce and a pinch of white pepper, as served at Singapore kopitiams.

nutrient_source.title: Half-Boiled Eggs — HPB Singapore Foodo Database, Food Code G00010236
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Exact match: HPB Foodo database food code G00010236 corresponds directly to Singapore-style half-boiled eggs (2 eggs). All per-100g nutrient values are sourced from this verified HPB record.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Eggs — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: N/A — food contains negligible carbohydrates. Eggs contain only approximately 1.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g; glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. The University of Sydney GI Database confirms eggs have no meaningful glycemic response. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by calculation. gi_category set to not_applicable per Veritas protocol for negligible-carbohydrate foods.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium from soy sauce drizzle (~166 mg per 2-egg serving, approximately 7% of the 2300 mg daily recommended limit)
hypertension.guidance: Singapore-style half-boiled eggs are low in sodium when consumed without excess soy sauce; individuals with hypertension should limit the soy sauce drizzle to 2–3 drops to keep the per-serving sodium contribution below 200 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate content (1.8 g per 100 g) with no meaningful glycemic response; high-quality protein and fat support satiety
type2_diabetes.guidance: Half-boiled eggs are one of the most diabetes-friendly breakfast options available — with essentially zero glycemic impact, high protein (11.2 g/100 g), and healthy fats that slow gastric emptying, they support stable postprandial blood glucose and are appropriate for daily consumption in a Type 2 Diabetes meal plan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High dietary cholesterol (365 mg per 100 g; approximately 475 mg per 2-egg serving) alongside moderate saturated fat (3.2 g/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While eggs are rich in cholesterol, current evidence (AHA 2019, meta-analyses) suggests moderate egg consumption (up to 1 egg per day) is acceptable for most cardiovascular patients; individuals with established CVD or familial hypercholesterolaemia should consult their clinician regarding frequency, as the cholesterol content of 2 eggs (~475 mg) exceeds the traditional 300 mg daily threshold.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Exceptional selenium (30 mcg/100 g), vitamin D (2.2 mcg/100 g), and vitamin B12 (1.02 mcg/100 g) — all clinically relevant for immune function and anti-inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Eggs are a complete protein source with micronutrients directly relevant to RA management; selenium and vitamin D support immune modulation, and regular egg consumption is consistent with anti-inflammatory dietary patterns recommended for rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Singapore-style half-boiled eggs (半熟蛋) are a beloved kopitiam (traditional coffee shop) institution and one of the most iconic breakfast items in Singapore's hawker culture. Two fresh eggs are lowered into a closed container of just-boiled water and left to steep for exactly 7–10 minutes, producing egg whites that are just barely set — translucent, silky, and barely opaque — while the yolks remain completely liquid, rich, and golden. The eggs are cracked open and slid into a small ceramic bowl, then seasoned immediately with a few drops of dark soy sauce (老抽, lao chou) and a generous pinch of white pepper. The result is a warm, custardy, umami-rich dish consumed in seconds. Nutritionally, 2 eggs (approximately 130 g) provide around 198 kcal, 14.6 g protein, 13.7 g fat, and only 2.3 g carbohydrate, making them an excellent high-protein, low-carbohydrate breakfast. They are rich in vitamin B12 (1.02 mcg/100 g), riboflavin (0.48 mg/100 g), selenium (30 mcg/100 g), vitamin D (2.2 mcg/100 g), and choline (via phosphorus 195 mg/100 g). The dish is always served as part of the classic Kaya Toast Set alongside kaya butter toast and a cup of kopi or teh. It is suitable for vegetarians, halal diets, and gluten-free diets. The high cholesterol content (365 mg/100 g) warrants moderation for individuals with cardiovascular risk factors, but the negligible carbohydrate content makes it ideal for people managing Type 2 Diabetes.
common_user_queries: how many calories in half-boiled eggs
how many eggs can I eat per day
is it safe to eat half-boiled eggs
half-boiled eggs vs hard-boiled eggs nutrition
are half-boiled eggs good for diabetes

confidence_reason: Exact match found in the HPB Singapore Foodo database under food code G00010236. All per-100g nutrient values are sourced directly from this verified, canonical HPB record. The dish is a well-documented Singapore hawker staple with consistent preparation and nutritional profile.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Singapore-style half-boiled eggs is not separately reported in the HPB Foodo database record G00010236; USDA FoodData Central (NDB 01129, whole egg cooked) reports approximately 3.8 g monounsaturated fat and 1.4 g polyunsaturated fat per 100 g as a reference estimate, but these values have not been adopted here to preserve source fidelity to the HPB record. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in HPB Foodo record G00010236. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB Foodo record G00010236. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero; eggs contain no dietary fibre (naturally zero). vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB Foodo record G00010236; eggs contain no vitamin C (naturally zero). Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (1.8 g/100 g); glycemic_index set to -1 (sentinel: not applicable for this food type) and gi_category set to not_applicable per Veritas protocol for negligible-carbohydrate foods. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0. The ethnic_tags field in the input incorrectly listed 'Japanese'; this has been corrected — the dish is Singaporean in origin. The occasion_tags in the input listed 'lunch' and 'dinner' but this dish is primarily a breakfast item; meal context corrected accordingly.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index set to -1 (numeric sentinel for not-applicable per Veritas protocol for negligible-carbohydrate foods — eggs contain only 1.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g). gi_category set to not_applicable. glycemic_load_per_serving set to 0.0. gi_source populated with University of Sydney GI Database reference and full explanation. glycemic_index is numeric as required by the scoring model.

Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. Additional condition rheumatoid_arthritis included in additional_conditions.

All other sections preserved from iteration 2 which passed all other gates and criteria.
